boardgames.headline: I think in systems. Boardgames keep that honest.
boardgames.body: Away from the keyboard, you'll find me at a table with a rulebook, a pile of cardboard, and strong opinions. I've been into boardgames for years — the kind where decisions compound, trade-offs are real, and the game punishes lazy thinking. I follow the scene, back the occasional Kickstarter, and have a shelf that's run out of room more than once.

boardgames_taste.headline: What I gravitate toward.
boardgames_taste.body: Mostly strategy and Euro-style games — the ones with elegant resource loops and meaningful choices on every turn. But I also have a soft spot for games that create tension and narrative on their own, without a GM in the room. If a game makes someone groan, laugh, or slam the table, it's doing something right.

items.title: Wyrmspan
items.note: The spiritual successor to Wingspan, but with dragons. What wins me over is how the engine-building feels loose and expressive early on, then snaps into focus by the final round. Every tableau tells a different story.

items.title: Nemesis
items.note: A semi-cooperative survival horror game where the real threat is often the other players. The asymmetry of objectives creates paranoia that no amount of rules can fully explain — you have to live it. One of the best 'experience' games in the hobby.

items.title: Terraforming Mars
items.note: The benchmark Euro for a reason. The card variety means no two games play the same, and the tension between personal engine and shared board state is a design lesson I keep coming back to.

boardgames_opinions.headline: A few things I actually believe.
items: Randomness is fine. Unmitigable randomness is lazy design.
A 90-minute game that feels tight beats a 4-hour game that meanders.
Solo modes are underrated — they're the best way to learn a system without burning a group's goodwill.

roleplaying.headline: Over 20 years behind the GM screen.
roleplaying.body: I've been running tabletop RPGs for over two decades. In that time I've shepherded campaigns through dungeon crawls, corporate heists, and hyperspace jumps — mostly from the GM's seat. There's something about building a world, populating it with problems, and then watching players find solutions you never anticipated that never gets old. It's the best kind of collaborative systems design.

rpg_systems.headline: Systems I've spent real time with.

items.title: Dungeons & Dragons
items.note: Where most people start, and for good reason. I've run everything from one-shots to multi-year campaigns. The system has its rough edges, but nothing else has the same shared language at the table.

items.title: Blades in the Dark / Powered by the Apocalypse
items.note: A different philosophy entirely — fiction-first, outcome-driven, collaborative by design. Running PbtA games taught me how much scaffolding traditional systems carry that you don't actually need. Blades in particular is a masterclass in elegant constraint.

items.title: Star Wars: Edge of the Empire
items.note: The narrative dice system shouldn't work as well as it does. Outcomes aren't just success or failure — they come with complications, advantages, and story beats baked in. It produces sessions that feel genuinely cinematic without trying too hard.

rpg_as_craft.headline: What GMing and engineering have in common.
rpg_as_craft.body: Both involve designing systems that people will interact with in ways you can't fully predict. The skill is the same: build enough structure to make the experience coherent, but leave enough space for the interesting stuff to happen. A good encounter and a good API have more in common than most people would admit.
